parentQuestion: A patient presents with severe acne. Examination reveals sebaceous glands that are overfilled with sebum, causing them to rupture and trigger inflammation. What mode of secretion characterizes these problematic glands?

scaffolds.explain: SEBACEOUS GLANDS are OIL-PRODUCING GLANDS that secrete SEBUM. Sebum lubricates and waterproofs your skin and hair.

wrongExplains.0: Sweat comes from eccrine and apocrine sweat glands.

options: Excess sebum production that clogs pores
scaffolds.explain: ACNE occurs when sebaceous glands produce TOO MUCH SEBUM. Excess oil + blocked pores = acne. The sebum has nowhere to go and gets trapped.

wrongExplains.0: Acne involves excess oil, not too little.
wrongExplains.3: Hormones stimulate sebum; excess hormones can worsen acne.

scaffolds.q: What happens to the cell in holocrine secretion?
options: The cell fills with product and ruptures to release it
Only the cell membrane is released
scaffolds.explain: In HOLOCRINE secretion, the cell FILLS WITH PRODUCT and RUPTURES to release it. The cell literally explodes — it dies in the process.

wrongExplains.2: The entire cell contents are released.
wrongExplains.3: Division before secretion doesn't define holocrine.

scaffolds.q: What type of secretion do sebaceous glands use?
options: Holocrine — cells rupture to release sebum
scaffolds.explain: Sebaceous glands use HOLOCRINE secretion — the cells RUPTURE to release sebum. Each sebaceous cell swells with oil until it bursts. Cellular martyrdom for soft skin.

wrongExplains.0: Merocrine keeps cells intact; sebaceous cells rupture.
wrongExplains.2: Apocrine only loses the apex; holocrine destroys the whole cell.
wrongExplains.3: Sebaceous glands secrete to skin surface, not blood.

scaffolds.q: How does merocrine secretion differ from holocrine?
options: Merocrine keeps the cell intact; holocrine destroys it
scaffolds.explain: In MEROCRINE secretion, the CELL STAYS INTACT — products are released by exocytosis. Sweat glands use merocrine. Sebaceous glands use holocrine (cell destruction).

scaffolds.q: How does apocrine secretion differ from holocrine?
options: Apocrine only pinches off the apex; holocrine destroys the whole cell
Apocrine is used by oil glands
scaffolds.explain: APOCRINE secretion PINCHES OFF part of the cell (the apex) but doesn't destroy it completely. HOLOCRINE destroys the ENTIRE CELL. Some mammary glands use apocrine.

wrongExplains.0: Apocrine only removes the apex; the cell survives.
wrongExplains.2: Oil glands use holocrine, not apocrine.

scaffolds.q: Why does holocrine secretion in acne create extra debris?
options: Cell rupture means dead cell debris mixes with sebum
The debris comes from outside the body
scaffolds.explain: In holocrine secretion, CELL RUPTURE creates DEAD CELL DEBRIS along with sebum. This debris gets trapped in blocked pores and feeds bacteria, worsening acne.

wrongExplains.2: Debris comes from dying cells inside.
wrongExplains.3: Cell debris exists before bacteria multiply.

scaffolds.q: Why do sebaceous glands need constant cell replacement?
options: Cells die every time they secrete (holocrine)
scaffolds.explain: Sebaceous glands need constant cell replacement because CELLS DIE EVERY TIME THEY SECRETE (holocrine). Stem cells at the base divide to replenish the population.

wrongExplains.0: Cells rupture in place; they don't migrate.
wrongExplains.2: Division replaces lost cells; it's not 'too fast.'

scaffolds.q: What mode of secretion characterizes sebaceous glands in acne?
scaffolds.explain: HOLOCRINE secretion characterizes sebaceous glands — cells rupture and die to release sebum. Not merocrine (cell stays intact), not apocrine (only apex lost), not endocrine (hormones into blood).

wrongExplains.0: Merocrine cells stay intact; sebaceous cells rupture.
wrongExplains.2: Apocrine only loses the apex; sebaceous cells die completely.
wrongExplains.3: Endocrine describes hormone secretion into blood.
